title: U.S. Marine Corps Year in Review
description: In 2025, Marines celebrated the legacy of global responsiveness and lethal combined arms passed down from the Marine Corps’ 1775 founding in Philadelphia.
text:
In 2025, Marines celebrated the legacy of global responsiveness and lethal combined arms passed down from the Marine Corps’ 1775 founding in Philadelphia. They took time to reflect on the service’s 250-year history while continuing to aggressively embrace its role as a naval expeditionary force.
Throughout 2025, that embrace was on display, as Marine Corps forces responded to events around the world—from humanitarian disasters in the Philippines to ongoing operations in the Caribbean. Meanwhile, exercises with allies, partners, and other U.S. services reinforced joint interoperability, regional commitments, and visible deterrence.
Refinement of Force Design continued. In October, the Commandant of the Marine Corps, General Eric M. Smith, announced a major decision: The Marine Corps will establish only two Marine littoral regiments (MLRs) instead of the three originally planned.1 In addition, the Marine Corps canceled its plan to field ground-based, long-range Tomahawk missile batteries. And in December, Secretary of the Navy John Phelan announced that a design from Dutch shipbuilder Damen would be the basis for the medium landing ship (LSM). Throughout the year, Marines tested advances in capabilities designed to ensure Marine air-ground task forces can outmatch adversaries in and out of the maritime domain.
Major challenges remain, however, particularly the continued poor state of amphibious ship readiness.
L-Class Readiness
General Smith has stated that the nation continues to require a 3.0 presence of amphibious ready groups (ARGs) and embarked Marine expeditionary units (MEUs).2 This goal requires one ARG/MEU deployed from the East Coast, one from the West Coast, and episodic patrols by the forward-deployed ARG/MEU based in Japan. This would enable Marines to meet a significant portion of combatant commanders’ needs. However, with the readiness of the 32 amphibious warships below 50 percent at present, the Navy’s capacity to support the goal looks unattainable. In May, then–Acting Chief of Naval Operations (CNO) Admiral James W. Kilby noted a combatant commander demand signal of 5.5 ARG/MEUs, but said the Navy “can only deliver 1.0 right now.” He emphasized, “We have to get back to 3.0 ARG/MEU presence.”3
While the Japan-based ARG/MEU conducted patrols during 2025, there was an eight-month gap in deployments from the continental United States—by units that, in theory, should meet 2.0 of the 3.0 objective. Before the Iwo Jima ARG/22nd MEU deployed from Norfolk, Virginia, in August, there were no deployments from either coast since December 2024, when the Boxer ARG/15th MEU returned to San Diego, California. In comparison with 2024, when the deployed presence of global U.S.-based ARG/MEUs was 1.10; in 2025, it cratered to below 0.4. By contrast, in the same timeframe, the deployment of U.S.-based carrier strike groups (CSGs) in 2025 stayed above 2.3.
In addition, an August fire on board the USS New Orleans (LPD-18) off Okinawa harmed the readiness of the forward-deployed amphibious forces in Japan. The fire took 12 hours to extinguish—harking back to the disastrous 2020 fire that caused the total loss of the USS Bon-homme Richard (LHD-6).4 Compounding the maintenance issue has been the delay in delivery of the next two America-class amphibious assault ships, the USS Bougainville (LHA-8) and Fallujah (LHA-9). Each delivery date is expected to slip by a year—to 2026 and 2030, respectively—because of “shipyard labor challenges.”5
In June, Admiral Kilby acknowledged to the House Armed Services Committee that the Navy has “done poorly” in completing maintenance on time and ensuring availability of amphibious warships. To remedy this problem, the Navy initiated a pilot program focused on reducing maintenance delays. It is developing required work packages for amphibious ship overhauls and availability periods earlier to enable more effective planning.6 In December, the new CNO, Admiral Daryl Caudle, issued a message to the Navy for a “World-class Fleet” that reinforced his predecessor’s goal for the fleet to achieve an 80 percent combat surge ready capability “for all platforms” (emphasis added).7
Being Operational
Starting in August, the Iwo Jima ARG and its embarked 22nd MEU operated in the Caribbean Sea alongside other U.S. naval forces, including the Gerald R. Ford CSG, supporting counternarcotics operations. During this time, 22nd MEU Marines also trained in Puerto Rico. As well, in the wake of the devastation wrought by Hurricane Melissa in October, some Marines from the 22nd supported the U.S. disaster response in Jamaica. In November, the Marines conducted training in Trinidad and Tobago, the island nation just a few miles off the coast of Venezuela.
Of note, the aviation combat element of the 22nd MEU includes AV-8B Harrier aircraft detached from Marine Attack Squadron 223, which in May became the last remaining operational U.S. Harrier squadron. This squadron will sundown the Harriers in 2026 as it transitions to the F-35B Lightning II.
In October, the II Marine Expeditionary Force (II MEF) was designated as the command structure for a joint task force (JTF) under U.S. Southern Command. Later designated JTF Operation Southern Spear, its ground, air, and logistics forces have been tasked to enhance and synchronize the ability of the United States and regional partners to “detect, disrupt, and dismantle illicit trafficking networks faster and at greater depth.”8
From March to October, Marine Rotational Force–Darwin (MRF-D) continued the annual six-month Australia training rotation begun in 2012. The 2,500-strong force conducted multiple training exercises in Australia, the Southwest Pacific, and Southeast Asia and responded to a variety of regional events and crises. These included Exercises Balikatan and Alon in the Philippines, Super Garuda Shield in Indonesia, and Talisman Saber and Southern Jackaroo in Australia.
Explosive ordnance disposal technicians from MRF-D participated in a multinational operation that removed more than 1,000 items of unexploded ordnance around Rabaul, Papua New Guinea. Elements of the force, including four MV-22Bs, took part in humanitarian assistance and disaster relief operations in Northern Luzon following a major typhoon. With the shortage of amphibious forces deploying from San Diego and Norfolk, MRF-D was able to mitigate commitment gaps by conducting and coordinating simultaneous operations in multiple countries. This included airfield insertion exercises in Palawan, the Philippines, and monitoring a French-sponsored exercise in New Caledonia, all while preparing for two major upcoming exercises in Australia.
Marines and aircraft also conducted a first for MRF-D by embarking and deploying for a period on board the expeditionary sea base USS Miguel Keith (ESB-5). This initiated a potentially innovative—if limited—aspect of future mobility given the dearth of amphibious lift. At the conclusion of this year’s deployment, MRF-D commander Colonel Jason Armas highlighted the achievement, saying, “We’re moving beyond interoperability to interchangeability. This rotation demonstrated our combined ability to rapidly project forces forward and uphold a free and prosperous Indo-Pacific.”
Also at the end of the 2025 rotation, the Australian government authorized the Marine Corps to leave its MV-22B Osprey tiltrotor aircraft in Australia for maintenance and storage between annual rotations. This will reduce transportation expenses and importation duty fees significantly while reducing wear and tear on the aircraft. The growing size of the Marine Corps forces in Australia reflects the increasing strategic prominence of the U.S.-Australian defense partnership and its importance for the broader Indo-Pacific.
During the springtime Exercise Balikatan, 3d MLR helped establish Littoral Rotational Force–Luzon (LRF-L). Soon after, the new force participated in Exercise Kamandag. Both exercises took advantage of 3d MLR’s experience over the past three years training with the Armed Forces of the Philippines and other allies and partners in coastal defense and other littoral operations. LRF-L’s initial experiences should provide insight to shape the composition and capabilities of future rotations.
Exercising at Scale
The Marine Corps conducted multiple large-scale exercises with allies and partners that played a critical role in fostering cooperation and integration as well as strengthening deterrence in dangerous areas of the globe. As the Commandant has emphasized, “These events sharpen readiness, validate emerging concepts, and accelerate the transition of new systems from experimentation to operational use.”9
Led by Marine Corps Forces Pacific, Balikatan fostered interoperability among the Marine Corps, the Japan Self-Defense Forces, and the Armed Forces of the Philippines. Third MLR employed the Navy–Marine Corps Expeditionary Ship Interdiction System (NMESIS) in simulated sea-denial operations across the strategic Luzon Strait. The Marine Air Defense Integrated System (MADIS) and AN/TPS-80 Ground/Air Task-Oriented Radar (G/ATOR) tested air-defense—including antidrone—operations.
Later in the spring, forces from the United States, the Philippines, Japan, South Korea, and the United Kingdom participated in Exercise Kamandag across the Philippine archipelago. Elements from both I and III MEF trained for mobile command and control of regional security, maritime interdiction, and combined-arms operations.
During the summer, Marine Corps forces from both Pacific-based MEFs participated in Talisman Saber in Australia. U.S. forces included the Japan-based America ARG and 31st MEU as well as MRF-D. The highlight of the exercise was simultaneous amphibious assault landings by U.S. Marines and forces from Australia, Japan, and South Korea, supported by 14 ships and expeditionary forces from six nations. The interoperability simulated combined capabilities for defense across the first island chain. The 31st MEU for the first time employed 8-by-8 wheeled Amphibious Combat Vehicles (ACVs) outside Okinawa. In addition, the MEU’s F-35Bs landed on, refueled, and launched from HMS Prince of Wales in the lead-up to Talisman Saber.
In September, III MEF and the Japan Self-Defense Forces conducted their annual bilateral Resolute Dragon exercise, which spanned from Hokkaido in the north to the Southwest Islands. The combined forces rehearsed expeditionary advanced base operations (EABO) focused on close kill webs and control and defense of key maritime terrain. This included simulated employment of III MEF’s NMESIS system. Also used were the Japan Ground Self-Defense Force Western Army’s Type 12 surface-to-ship missile and the U.S. Army’s Typhon missile system.
Throughout the year, Marine Corps and Japanese forces increased their activity and presence on Japan’s Southwestern Islands. In October, III MEF’s 1st Marine Air Wing established a forward arming and refueling point on Yonaguni Island, only 70 miles from Taiwan—where China has increased manned and unmanned aircraft incursions into Japanese airspace. These efforts align with recent comments by Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth and the recently published National Security Strategy that call for increased capabilities, access, and cooperation with partners and allies “to deter adversaries and protect the First Island Chain.”10
On the U.S. East Coast in early summer, Marines participated in Atlantic Alliance 25, formerly known as Bold Alligator. More than 8,500 service members from the U.S. Navy, II MEF, the Marine Corps Reserve, and allies from the Netherlands, United Kingdom, and Canada participated. It was the largest amphibious exercise in the western Atlantic in more than a decade, with naval maneuver exercises conducted from North Carolina to Maine. It focused on integrating allied capabilities, with an emphasis on littoral warfare in a medium-to-high-threat environment.
Force Design Is Never Done
In October, the Commandant issued the latest Force Design update.11 New and revised elements are informed by the Marine Corps’ “continuous Campaign of Learning,” which includes analysis and testing as well as “lessons drawn from contemporary battlefields.” The document focuses on adapting faster than the enemy—particularly with uncrewed systems, precision and long-range fires, and cyber and electronic warfare.
A particularly noteworthy update is the decision to reduce from three to two the number of MLRs. Based on field experiments, lessons from major exercises, and other analyses, 4th Marine Regiment will be retained as a reinforced Marine infantry regiment. The document says, “Two MLRs and one reinforced Marine Infantry Regiment in III MEF is the optimal force composition to meet III MEF’s missions and objectives.” Shortly after the update was issued, three former Commandants—Generals Charles C. Krulak, Michael W. Hagee, and James T. Conway—published an article critiquing Force Design. They believe the changes in force composition are undermining the Marine Corps’ “combined arms capabilities and resilience.”12
A key concept the Commandant and Acting CNO signed in 2025 is the classified Concept for 21st Century Amphibious Operations. According to open-source information, this document describes the full range of potential future amphibious operations needed to meet the particular dilemmas found in contested maritime and littoral environments. It articulates the future role of amphibious operations and explains the employment of long-range unmanned systems to infiltrate the adversary’s weapon engagement zone as well as the incorporation of new, agile platforms to supplement traditional amphibious ships. It also considers the use of dispersed formations of manned and unmanned ships that challenge adversary targeting and complement traditional amphibious ships and the adoption of disruptive technologies. It further explains how Marines should integrate with naval, joint, allied, and partner forces to shape and sustain distributed combat operations.
Medium Landing Ship
In December, Secretary Phelan announced the selection of Dutch shipbuilder Damen’s LST-100 as the baseline for the medium landing ship. The Secretary emphasized that this was part of the administration’s efforts to revamp U.S. naval shipbuilding. He said the choice of this proven design was “an operationally-driven, fiscally-disciplined choice that puts capability in the fleet on a responsible timeline.”13 A U.S. vessel construction manager will be selected soon to oversee the program.
The LSM will displace about 4,000 tons and be 328 feet long, with a crew of 18. It will have a range of 3,400 nautical miles and a maximum speed of 15 knots. It will retain the LST-100’s ability to beach, unloading from either the doors in the bow or the stern ramp. It will have the capacity for 234 troops, 11,000 square feet of cargo space, and a small helicopter landing area. Australia also has selected this design as the basis for its future heavy landing craft requirement, and Damen has already delivered an LST-100 to the Nigerian Navy.
The exact number of LSMs to be constructed is not yet firm, with estimates ranging from 18 to 35 ships. In a video announcement of the selection, the Commandant said the LSM “will enable our Marines to be more agile and flexible in austere environments where there are no ports, providing the joint force the needed operational mobility within the adversary’s weapons engagement zone.” Of note, these ships will not be counted as part of the congressionally mandated 31 amphibious warships that include at least 10 big decks (LHAs/LHDs).
No projected delivery date for the first LSM has been announced. In the meantime, the Marine Corps continues to employ its “littoral maneuver bridging strategy” as an interim solution to fill the gap in mobility within the first island chain. The short-term solutions include expeditionary fast transports, landing craft utility, and maritime prepositioning ships to support dispersed EABs. In addition, the Marine Corps purchased two ancillary surface craft–medium vessels from the Australian firm Birdon to continue refining concepts and doctrine.
In the Mud
Informed by Marine Corps Warfighting Laboratory experiments, the service is creating a fires and reconnaissance company within infantry battalions. This unit will integrate manned and unmanned intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) assets with fires that include organic precision munitions, which should be fielded starting in 2026. In addition, the Force Design update says the Marine Corps will revert to a rifle squad size of 13 Marines—four fire teams led by a school-trained sergeant. MLRs at one point developed 14-Marine squads of fewer but larger fire teams.
In January, the Marine Corps Attack Drone Team stood up at Quantico under the Marine Corps Weapons Training Battalion.14 In response to lessons from the war in Ukraine, the team will build on the Marine Corps’ marksmanship culture to accelerate the integration of first-person view, one-way attack drones—aerial vehicles/weapons that transmit a live feed to a remote operator who can control precise targeting and timing. The team also will provide timely feedback on small unmanned aerial systems (sUASs) and counter-UAS requirements. Initial insights suggest the Marine Corps will nest attack-drone operators in combat units across the force.
Light armed reconnaissance battalions are evolving to become mobile reconnaissance battalions. The latter will include a new maritime reconnaissance company (MRC) that will operate new tactical boats: multimission reconnaissance craft (MMRCs). The Marine Corps has been experimenting with an Australian-developed MMRC that will work with uncrewed surface vessels to maneuver sensors and personnel in the littorals, insert and extract Marines, perform ISR, conduct target acquisition, and extend sensor coverage across complex terrain. The number of MMRCs in each MRC has not yet been determined.
Development of the advanced reconnaissance vehicle (ARV)—the replacement for the light armored vehicle—continues with increasing urgency. The ARV will be equipped with advanced sensors and communication systems while providing greater littoral mobility.
The Marine Corps canceled the Long Range Fires (LRF) program. Built around a single Navy Mk 41 vertical-launch cell, the LRF would have been capable of firing both land-attack and maritime-strike Tomahawk missiles.15 After LRF testing identified concerns about maneuverability, the Marine Corps decided to stick with NMESIS. That system will support sea-control operations using the Naval Strike Missile, which has an advertised range of more than 162 nautical miles. In addition, the Marine Corps will procure the Army-developed precision-strike missile (PrSM) for High Mobility Artillery Rocket System (HIMARS) batteries. The pending land-based antiship PrSM Increment 2 is projected to have a range of at least 620 miles.
Up in the Air
The January 2025 Marine Corps Aviation Plan laid out how the service’s platforms, personnel, and operating concepts should evolve.16 It announced the addition of aviation ground systems (AGS) as the seventh core function of Marine aviation.17 Another key concept paper was released as well: the Functional Concept for Distributed Aviation Operations (DAO), which considers how to improve aviation capabilities to support EABO and stand-in force operations. In support of the functional concept for distributed aviation operations, “AGS units are trained and equipped to establish and sustain DAO nodes with minimal infrastructure, reinforcing the Marine Corps’ ability to operate from austere locations.” The aviation plan highlights the significant AGS modernization efforts to make Marine Corps aviation a critical enabler for EABO.
The service also is working to reinvigorate its ground-based air defense. Bolstered by lessons from Ukraine, the Marine Corps continues to roll out a multilayered system. The Medium Range Intercept Capability (MRIC) defends critical, operationally fixed assets against cruise missiles, aircraft, drones, and mortars out to 43 miles. Incorporating Iron Dome technology, MRIC integrates with the Marine Corps Ground/Air Task-Oriented Radar and the Common Aviation Command and Control System. Full-rate production of MRIC began in 2025. Beginning in fiscal year (FY) 2026, active air wings will be equipped with a full MRIC battery, with all having one by FY2028.18
The Marine Air Defense Integrated System consists of a complementary pair of Joint Light Tactical Vehicle–mounted weapon systems. One carries Stinger missiles, a 30-mm gun, an electronic warfare (EW) capability, and optics. The other, a counter-UAS variant, will possess EW capability, radar, the same 30-mm gun, and communication systems. The first 20 have been fielded, and full-rate production began in September. The light version, L-MADIS, is being developed to support more mobile operations, and it is mounted “on a pair of ultra-light tactical vehicles (ULTVs).” The first 10 production L-MADIS systems will be fielded later in 2026.
Other major aviation milestones during 2025 included the reactivation of Marine Medium Tiltrotor Squadron 264 in December. This MV-22B Osprey unit was deactivated in 2020 but is now being reestablished as a result of lessons learned over the past five years. Other Osprey squadrons demonstrated a capability to deploy sonobuoys and integrate Marine Corps aircraft into antisubmarine warfare operations with tests in both the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
The F-35 program continues, with the transition of 11 operational F/A-18C/D and AV-8B squadrons and 2 training squadrons completed; four additional squadrons are still in transition. In addition, the Marine Corps is experimenting with pairing F-35 aircraft with the XQ-58 Valkyrie Combat Collaborative Aircraft to evaluate manned-unmanned teaming to expand aviation reach and survivability.
Finally, the aviation plan announced that the Marine Corps and Navy are developing a replacement for the two aviation support ships (T-AVBs), which are more than 55 years old. A key requirement for the T-AVB “Next” class will be the ability to support both aviation and ground maintenance, a Marine Corps seabasing sustainment capability goal since the late 1960s.19
Logistics Is the Backbone
Logistics will be contested in every clime and place, especially in the austere environments in which Marines expect to fight. As a result, the Marine Corps is expanding its global prepositioning network, particularly in the Indo-Pacific, to enable more resilient sustainment. New sites are planned for the Philippines, Australia, and Palau that will augment the current site in Norway as well as the afloat Maritime Prepositioning Force. In February, the Marine Corps leased a 57,000-square-foot warehouse at the former Subic Bay Naval Supply Depot. This site will store vehicles, communication equipment, and engineering assets.
The Marine Corps has also started testing an autonomous low-profile vessel (ALPV), sometimes referred to as a “narco-sub.” The ALPV is an unmanned surface craft that should provide more survivable long-range logistics distribution, in part through reduced detectability. It can be configured to deliver multiple variations of supplies and equipment throughout the littorals. The October Force Design Update says the project is transitioning to a program of record.
The service began fielding the Tactical Resupply Unmanned Aircraft System (TRUAS), which reached initial operational capability in November. TRUAS enhances routine distribution to small units and can carry payloads of up to 150 pounds as far as nine miles.
The Marine Corps is also experimenting with two larger UAS resupply systems. The Medium Aerial Resupply Vehicle–Expeditionary Logistics has a requirement for a specified payload between 300 and 600 pounds and an operational radius of 25 to 100 nautical miles. The competition was reopened in May after a 2024 competition did not result in an acceptable prototype. In addition, the Aerial Logistics Connector program is testing unmanned versions of proven manned helicopters to complement the smaller resupply aircraft.
Experimentation and Other Enhancements
Keeping pace with the rapid advance of technology, in May the Marine Corps released an artificial intelligence (AI) implementation plan with goals and timelines. These include developing AI use cases across all warfighting functions, deploying digital implementation teams, developing AI competency across the force, and including data-centricity in inspection programs.
In September, the Marine Corps signed a new license with Palantir Technologies to employ the company’s AI-powered Maven Smart System (MSS) down to the tactical level. MSS is expected to enable more rapid sensor-to-shooter targeting, enhance digital flow and maritime domain awareness, and improve decision-making.
Also in September, then-Assistant Commandant of the Marine Corps, General Christopher J. Mahoney, signed a memorandum establishing Project Dynamis. This is an initiative to accelerate the modernization of Marine Corps contributions to Combined Joint All-Domain Command and Control in partnership with the Department of the Navy’s Project Overmatch. A key goal is to develop “end-to-end, joint interoperable capabilities that enable Marines to act as the forward element of the Joint Force—sensing, making sense, and communicating weapons quality data at the speed and scale of relevance.” In a related effort, III MEF Information Group led a “first of a kind” combined, joint, and interagency exercise in May on Okinawa. It tested cyber operations, electronic warfare, and multidomain sensing to coordinate targeting and effects in distributed operations.20
‘We Make Marines’
The Marine Corps ended the year strongly, exceeding its 30,535 FY2025 active-duty and reserve enlisted recruiting goal by a single recruit.21 While the narrow victory might appear to be concerning, Marine Corps officials credit the close call to greater selectivity in whom it recruits along with effective planning. The Marine Corps also met its 2026 retention goals shortly after the fiscal year began. While it is not unusual to achieve this milestone close to when the fiscal year window opens, the Commandant said this goal was met “earlier than ever before.”22
Marines concluded their anniversary year by adding to the Marine Corps’ rich heritage. The highlights of 2025 were not battles won and honors earned. Instead, they were the service’s efforts to prepare for whatever battles must be deterred and those that will be fought. Marines’ commitment to and skill in warfighting demand a focus on readiness; tough, realistic training; and the capacity to harness technology in the face of battlefield uncertainty. All were demonstrated throughout 2025 by the continual evaluation and reevaluation of concepts, doctrine, and execution that are a hallmark of the Marine Corps. Force Design, amphibious ship readiness, and new technologies and capabilities have each created challenges, but the service’s 250-year-long culture of innovation and adaptation have prepared Marines to meet them.
